Force a refresh: mvn clean install -U (or Studio's Update project dependencies)
Delete the project and start over
optionNotes: Correct — typo'd versions/coordinates are the first suspect.
Correct — unreachable or unauthenticated repositories cause resolution failures, especially behind mirrors.
Correct — -U retries cached failed lookups (Maven caches 'not found').
Wrong — the project isn't the problem; resolution config is.
questions.explanation: Dependency triage: coordinates/version → repository reachability/credentials (settings.xml) → -U to bust the negative cache.

questions.q: Given the two property sources shown and a deployment with -Dhttp.port=9090, what is the value of ${http.port} at runtime?
questions.exhibit:
config/dev.yaml (loaded via configuration-properties):
http:
  port: "8081"

Deployment command:
mvn ... -Denv=dev -Dhttp.port=9090
options: 9090 — system properties passed with -D override values from property files
8081 — files always win
Both are used on alternating requests
Deployment fails due to the conflict
optionNotes: Correct — precedence puts deployment/system properties above configuration files: 9090 wins.
Wrong — files are the DEFAULTS that higher-precedence sources override.
Wrong — properties resolve once at startup, deterministically.
Wrong — overriding is by design, not a conflict.
questions.explanation: Property precedence (high→low): deployment/system properties → environment-specific sources → packaged files. This enables build-once-deploy-many.

questions.q: How does an application select dev.yaml vs prod.yaml at deploy time with ONE packaged artifact?
questions.exhibit: <configuration-properties file="config/${env}.yaml"/>
options: The file name is parameterized with ${env}; each deployment passes -Denv=dev or -Denv=prod
Both files load and merge automatically
The runtime detects the environment from the hostname
Two different JARs must be built
optionNotes: Correct — the properties FILE PATH itself is a placeholder resolved from the env system/deployment property.
Wrong — only the resolved file loads; there is no automatic merge of all environment files.
Wrong — no hostname magic exists; the property must be provided.
Wrong — defeating build-once is exactly what this pattern avoids.
questions.explanation: config/${env}.yaml + -Denv=<name> is the canonical environment-selection idiom — one artifact, environment decided at deployment.

questions.q: A flow must reference the property db.host inside a DataWeave transform (not a config field). Which expression is correct?
options: p('db.host')
${db.host}
vars.db.host
properties.db.host
optionNotes: Correct — the Mule::p() function reads configuration properties from DataWeave.
Wrong — ${} placeholders work in COMPONENT FIELDS (resolved at startup), not inside DataWeave scripts.
Wrong — properties are not variables.
Wrong — there is no 'properties' binding in DataWeave.
questions.explanation: Two access paths: ${prop} in XML attribute values; p('prop') inside DataWeave expressions/scripts.

questions.q: An app has apis.xml (flows + HTTP Listener referencing 'httpConfig') and global.xml (the httpConfig listener config). What makes this work, and why structure it this way?
options: All XML files in src/main/mule are combined into one application at deployment; separating globals centralizes reusable configuration
apis.xml must import global.xml with an <import> tag or it fails
It only works if the files are alphabetically ordered
Global elements must be redefined in every file that uses them
optionNotes: Correct — the runtime merges every configuration file; cross-file references (config-ref) resolve app-wide. Dedicated global.xml is a readability/reuse convention.
Wrong — no import mechanism is needed between an app's own config files.
Wrong — file naming/order does not affect resolution.
Wrong — one definition serves the whole application.
questions.explanation: An app's XML files form ONE configuration at deploy time; global elements defined anywhere are referenceable everywhere — hence the global.xml convention.

questions.q: Which execution model differences apply when calling reusable logic via Flow Reference to a SUBFLOW vs posting to a VM queue consumed by another flow? (Select all that apply)
options: Subflow call: synchronous, same event, caller waits for the result
VM publish (one-way): asynchronous, a NEW event is created for the consuming flow
Variables cross the VM queue automatically
Both behave identically
optionNotes: Correct — Flow References are in-line synchronous invocations sharing the event.
Correct — VM decouples: the consumer gets a new event (payload transferred; attributes/vars do not follow).
Wrong — the transport boundary strips vars/attributes; only the payload (and configured properties) crosses.
Wrong — synchronicity, event identity, and error propagation all differ.
questions.explanation: Flow Reference = synchronous call semantics. VM = messaging semantics (async decoupling, new event, no vars). Choosing between them is an architecture decision, not syntax.

questions.q: Which project location holds DataWeave module files, property files, and other classpath resources?
options: src/main/resources
src/main/mule
src/test/munit
target/classes
optionNotes: Correct — resources (yaml/properties, DW libraries, schemas, examples) live in src/main/resources.
Wrong — src/main/mule holds the Mule configuration XMLs.
Wrong — that's the MUnit test suite folder.
Wrong — target/ is generated build output, never source.
questions.explanation: Project layout: src/main/mule (flows), src/main/resources (classpath resources), src/test/munit (tests), pom.xml + mule-artifact.json (build/descriptor).

questions.q: Two teams each own a Mule app deployed to the SAME customer-hosted runtime, and both need to listen on port 8443 with the same TLS certificate. What enables this?
options: A Mule domain project defining the shared HTTP Listener config (with TLS); both apps reference the domain
Both apps define listeners on 8443 — the runtime multiplexes automatically
Deploying one app inside the other
This requires CloudHub
optionNotes: Correct — domains share global configs (listener/port/TLS) across apps on one runtime; both apps bind through the shared listener.
Wrong — two independent binds on one port collide (Address already in use).
Wrong — apps don't nest.
Wrong — CloudHub doesn't even support domains; this is a customer-hosted feature.
questions.explanation: Domains = shared resources for co-located apps on customer-hosted runtimes: one port, one TLS config, many apps. Not available on CloudHub (isolated workers).

questions.q: What is the effect of marking properties as secure and referencing them as shown?
questions.exhibit:
config file:
db:
  password: "![jd8sK2n...==]"

flow config field:
password="${secure::db.password}"
options: The encrypted value is decrypted at runtime with a key supplied at deployment; the secure:: prefix is required for properties in the secure configuration
The password is stored in plain text with a marker
secure:: makes Mule hash the value
The syntax is invalid
optionNotes: Correct — ![...] wraps the encrypted value; the Secure Configuration Properties module decrypts using the runtime-provided key; secure:: is the mandatory access prefix.
Wrong — the value is ciphertext, not marked plaintext.
Wrong — decryption (reversible with the key), not hashing.
Wrong — this is exactly the secure-properties syntax.
questions.explanation: Secure properties basics (deep-dive in MCD L2): encrypt values as ![...], reference via ${secure::...}, supply the key at deploy time — never packaged.

questions.q: An API on CloudHub must keep serving requests while a worker is restarted by the platform, and must handle 3× current load next quarter. Which deployment change addresses BOTH?
options: Scale horizontally to multiple workers — automatic load balancing provides both availability during restarts and added throughput capacity
One much larger worker
Enable persistent queues
Move the app to another region
optionNotes: Correct — 2+ workers = surviving instances keep serving (HA) AND capacity scales with count.
Wrong — a single worker of any size is still a single point of failure during restarts.
Wrong — persistent queues protect QUEUED MESSAGES, not HTTP availability.
Wrong — region choice affects latency/residency, not HA within the deployment.
questions.explanation: Horizontal scaling on CloudHub answers both availability (workers fail independently) and throughput (linear capacity for stateless apps).

questions.q: The exhibit shows an autodiscovery element. The API instance in API Manager stays 'Unregistered' after deploying to CloudHub. Which causes should be investigated? (Select all that apply)
questions.exhibit:
<api-gateway:autodiscovery
    apiId="${api.id}"
    flowRef="orders-main"/>
options: The api.id property resolves to the WRONG environment's instance id
The environment's Anypoint Platform client_id/client_secret are not configured for the deployment
The flowRef doesn't reference the flow containing the HTTP listener + router
The RAML has a typo in a documentation string
optionNotes: Correct — instance ids are per environment; a dev id in prod pairs with nothing.
Correct — the runtime authenticates to the platform with environment credentials to register.
Correct — autodiscovery must point at the main (routed) flow.
Wrong — spec documentation has no effect on gateway pairing.
questions.explanation: Autodiscovery pairing checklist: correct per-environment api.id + platform client credentials on the runtime + flowRef to the main flow. Miss any and the instance stays Unregistered.

questions.q: A rate-limiting policy must change from 100 to 500 requests/minute on a production API. What is required?
options: Edit the policy configuration on the API instance in API Manager — no application redeployment; the gateway picks up the change
Rebuild and redeploy the Mule application
Edit the RAML and re-scaffold
Restart the CloudHub workers
optionNotes: Correct — policies are managed configuration: applied, changed, and removed at the instance level and pulled by gateways at runtime.
Wrong — policy config lives outside the application artifact.
Wrong — the spec documents the API; it doesn't carry runtime policy settings.
Wrong — no restart is needed for policy updates.
questions.explanation: Separation of concerns: implementation (deployed artifact) vs governance (API Manager policies). Policy changes are zero-deployment operations.

questions.q:
Order the steps a NEW consumer follows to call a production API governed with client ID enforcement and SLA tiers:
(A) receive client_id/secret  (B) find the API in Exchange  (C) request access with a client app + tier  (D) send credentials on each request
options: B → C → A → D
A → B → C → D
C → B → D → A
D → C → B → A
optionNotes: Correct — discover (Exchange) → request access (client app + tier → contract) → credentials issued → use them per the policy's configured mechanism.
Wrong — credentials don't exist before the access request creates the client app/contract.
Wrong — access is requested against an API you first discover.
Wrong — you cannot call before having credentials.
questions.explanation: The consumer journey: Exchange discovery → access request (tier/contract) → credentials → invocation. The gateway then enforces identity + tier per contract.

questions.q: What does 'mvn clean package' produce for a Mule 4 project, and where?
options: A deployable application JAR in the target/ directory (after running MUnit tests)
A WAR file in dist/
A ZIP in src/main/mule
Nothing without -DmuleDeploy
optionNotes: Correct — package builds the deployable archive (mule-application JAR) into target/, with the test phase running first.
Wrong — Mule 4 artifacts are JARs, not WARs, and output goes to target/.
Wrong — src/ is source; build output never lands there.
Wrong — -DmuleDeploy affects the DEPLOY phase, not packaging.
questions.explanation: mvn clean package = tests + deployable JAR in target/ — the artifact that deploys unchanged to CloudHub, Runtime Fabric, or customer-hosted runtimes.

questions.q: Which platform component and mechanism HIDE a sensitive property value that operators set on a CloudHub application?
options: Runtime Manager properties marked as hidden/protected (and listing the property in mule-artifact.json secureProperties masks it in the UI/API)
Encrypting the JAR file
Exchange access controls
Setting the property in the RAML
optionNotes: Correct — deployment properties can be hidden in Runtime Manager; secureProperties in mule-artifact.json masks named keys from display.
Wrong — artifact encryption isn't a platform feature and wouldn't affect property display.
Wrong — Exchange governs assets, not runtime properties.
Wrong — specs don't carry deployment configuration.
questions.explanation: Sensitive deploy-time values: set them as hidden Runtime Manager properties and/or list keys in mule-artifact.json's secureProperties so the platform masks them.

sections.title: Explaining Application Network Basics
objectives: Explain MuleSoft's proposal for closing the IT delivery gap
Describe the characteristics and benefits of modern APIs and API-led connectivity
Describe the role of a Center for Enablement (C4E)
Define the basic elements of HTTP requests and responses
Describe the capabilities and high-level components of Anypoint Platform
sections.notes:
The IT delivery gap

Business demand on IT grows every year (new channels, SaaS apps, mobile, IoT), but IT delivery capacity stays roughly flat when every project is a custom point-to-point integration. Point-to-point creates tight coupling: every new connection is bespoke code, changes ripple unpredictably, and nothing is reusable. The gap between what the business asks for and what IT can deliver keeps widening.

MuleSoft's proposal: build an application network — a network of applications, data, and devices connected with reusable, discoverable, self-serve APIs. Key properties of an application network:

  • It is built incrementally, project by project ("bendable, not breakable"). Each project consumes existing APIs and contributes new ones, so delivery gets faster over time.
  • Nodes are loosely coupled: consumers depend on API contracts, not implementations, so implementations can change without breaking consumers.
  • Assets are discoverable and self-serve through Anypoint Exchange — teams find and reuse instead of rebuilding.
  • It is recomposable: existing APIs are rewired into new products and channels quickly.

API-led connectivity — the three layers

  • System APIs — mediate access to a core system (one system, canonical data model). They hide complexity (SOAP, JDBC, proprietary formats) and shield consumers from system changes/migrations.
  • Process APIs — encapsulate business processes that interact with and shape data across systems (e.g. "create order" touching CRM + ERP). They are independent of both the source systems and the delivery channels.
  • Experience APIs — reconfigure data so it is easily consumed by its intended audience/channel, without duplicating process logic per channel.

Exam tip: given a scenario ("an API that aggregates customer data from three systems for a mobile app"), be able to place each API in the correct layer. Aggregation/orchestration → Process; channel-specific shaping → Experience; direct system unlock → System.

Modern APIs

MuleSoft describes modern APIs as having three characteristics: they are treated as products (designed for and marketed to consumers, with a lifecycle), they are discoverable and self-describing (documentation, specs, examples in Exchange), and they are managed (secured, monitored, governed with policies and SLAs).

Center for Enablement (C4E)

A C4E is a cross-functional team (IT + line-of-business members) that enables the rest of the organization instead of delivering everything itself:

  • Builds and publishes reusable assets (APIs, templates, examples, best-practice guides) as products.
  • Establishes and evangelizes best practices; supports and trains project teams.
  • Measures consumption of assets (KPIs like reuse rate) — the network's value grows with reuse.

Contrast with a traditional Center of Excellence (CoE): a CoE centralizes delivery ("do it for the teams"), which becomes a bottleneck; a C4E decentralizes delivery and centralizes enablement.

HTTP fundamentals

MethodPurposeHas body?Idempotent?
GETRetrieve a resource/collectionNoYes (safe)
POSTCreate a resource / process dataYesNo
PUTReplace a resource at a known URI (create-or-replace)YesYes
PATCHPartially update a resourceYesNo (in general)
DELETERemove a resourceNoYes

An HTTP request = method + URI (+ query string) + protocol version + headers + optional body. An HTTP response = status line + headers + optional body. Common status codes:

CodeMeaning
200 / 201 / 202 / 204OK / Created / Accepted (async) / No Content
301 / 304Moved Permanently / Not Modified
400 / 401 / 403 / 404 / 405 / 415 / 429Bad Request / Unauthorized (not authenticated) / Forbidden (not authorized) / Not Found / Method Not Allowed / Unsupported Media Type / Too Many Requests (rate limit)
500 / 502 / 503 / 504Internal Server Error / Bad Gateway / Service Unavailable / Gateway Timeout

Anypoint Platform components

AreaComponentWhat it does
DesignDesign Center (API Designer)Web tool to write API specifications (RAML/OAS) and build fragments; includes the mocking service
Anypoint Studio / Code BuilderDesktop/cloud IDEs to implement, test, and debug Mule applications
Share & discoverAnypoint ExchangeCatalog of reusable assets: specs, fragments, connectors, templates, examples, custom policies; generates API portals and REST Connect connectors
Manage (control plane)API ManagerCreate/manage API instances, apply policies, SLA tiers, contracts, alerts, proxies
Runtime ManagerDeploy, manage, scale, and monitor applications on any deployment target
Access ManagementUsers, roles, permissions, business groups, environments, connected apps
Anypoint Monitoring / VisualizerDashboards, logs, alerts / real-time visualization of the application network
Run (runtime plane)Mule runtimeRuns apps on CloudHub (MuleSoft-hosted), Runtime Fabric (containers), or customer-hosted servers

Exam tip: know which component does what. "Where do consumers discover and request access to an API?" → Exchange. "Where are policies applied?" → API Manager. "Where do you scale workers?" → Runtime Manager.

sections.title: Designing and Consuming APIs
objectives: Describe the lifecycle of the modern API (design, simulate, feedback, validate)
Use RAML to define API resources, nested resources, and methods
Identify when to use query parameters vs URI parameters
Use RAML to define request/response bodies, headers, and status codes
Use RAML to define reusable data types and format-independent examples
Formulate RESTful requests from a RAML API specification
sections.notes:
The design-first API lifecycle

Modern APIs are designed before they are implemented: design → simulate → get feedback → validate, iterating until consumers agree the contract is right. Only then is the API implemented, tested, deployed, and managed. Tools per stage:

  • Design — API Designer (Design Center) writing RAML or OAS.
  • Simulate — the mocking service serves responses straight from the spec's examples, giving consumers a live endpoint before any code exists.
  • Feedback — publish to Exchange; consumers explore the auto-generated API console/portal and comment.
  • Validate — refine the spec until it is stable; it becomes the contract that APIkit later enforces.

RAML essentials

#%RAML 1.0
title: American Flights API
version: v1
baseUri: http://localhost:8081/api

types:
  AmericanFlight: !include dataTypes/american-flight.raml

/flights:
  get:
    queryParameters:
      destination:
        required: false
        enum: [SFO, LAX, CLE]
    responses:
      200:
        body:
          application/json:
            type: AmericanFlight[]
            examples: !include examples/AmericanFlightsExample.raml
  post:
    body:
      application/json:
        type: AmericanFlight
    responses:
      201:
        body:
          application/json:
            example: {"message": "Flight added"}
  /{ID}:
    get:
      responses:
        200:
          body:
            application/json:
              type: AmericanFlight
    put:
    delete:
  • RAML is YAML-based: indentation defines nesting, and it is case-sensitive.
  • Resources begin with /; nesting a resource under another creates a sub-path (/flights/{ID}).
  • URI parameters use {curlyBraces} and identify a specific resource. Query parameters are declared under a method's queryParameters: and filter/sort/paginate collections. Rule of thumb: identity → URI parameter; refinement of a result set → query parameter.
  • Each method can declare headers:, body: (per media type), and responses: (per status code, each with its own body/type/example).

Reuse in RAML

MechanismScopeExample use
types: / DataType fragmentData structurestype: AmericanFlight shared by requests and responses
NamedExample fragmentExample instancesOne example file reused by several methods (format-independent)
traits: (applied with is:)Method-level patternsPagination query params, standard error responses, auth headers
resourceTypes: (applied with type:)Resource-level patternsStandard "collection" and "member" resource shapes
Library (uses:)Bundle of types/traits/etc.Common enterprise definitions shared across specs
Overlay / ExtensionWhole specAdd annotations/translations (overlay) or environment additions (extension) without touching the master spec

Fragments (#%RAML 1.0 DataType, Trait, NamedExample…) can live in separate files included with !include, or be published to Exchange as reusable assets and imported into many API specs as dependencies.

A RAML data type defines fields, types, and constraints:

#%RAML 1.0 DataType
type: object
properties:
  ID: integer
  code:
    type: string
    pattern: "[A-Z]{3}\\d{3}"
  price:
    type: number
    required: false

Consuming APIs

  • To call an API from a spec: match method + full path (baseUri + resource) + required parameters/headers/body. E.g. from the spec above: GET http://localhost:8081/api/flights?destination=SFO or POST /api/flights with a JSON AmericanFlight body.
  • When a spec is published to Exchange, REST Connect automatically generates a REST Connector (one operation per method) that can be dragged into a Mule flow — an alternative to configuring raw HTTP Request operations.
  • The API console (in Design Center or Exchange) and the mocking service let anyone try requests without an implementation.

Exam tip: expect questions that show a RAML snippet and ask which request is valid (correct path nesting, query vs URI parameter, required vs optional). Read the indentation carefully — it defines what belongs to what.

sections.title: Accessing and Modifying Mule Events
objectives: Describe the Mule event data structure (message payload, attributes, variables)
Use transformers to set the payload, attributes, and variables
Write DataWeave expressions to access and modify payload, attributes, and variables
Enrich Mule events using target parameters
sections.notes:
Anatomy of a Mule event

  • Payload — the body/data being processed. Can be any type: JSON, XML, Java object, stream…
  • Attributes — metadata that arrived with the message. For an HTTP Listener: attributes.queryParams, attributes.uriParams, attributes.headers, attributes.method, attributes.requestPath. For a File listener: file name, size, timestamps.
  • Variables (vars) — data you attach to the event; they live for the rest of the flow and travel through Flow References.

The Mule event is immutable — every change creates a new event instance behind the scenes. Practically, this means each processor receives the event produced by the previous one.

The golden rule of attributes

Every time the event passes through a connector operation (HTTP Request, Database Select, File Read…), the message is replaced: the payload becomes the operation's result AND the attributes are replaced with metadata about that result (e.g. HTTP response status and headers). If you need the original query params later, save them to a variable first.

Accessing event data with DataWeave

payload                              // whole payload
payload.customer.lastName            // field navigation
payload[0].price                     // array index
attributes.queryParams.destination   // ?destination=SFO
attributes.uriParams.ID              // /flights/{ID}
attributes.headers.'client_id'       // request header
vars.myVar                           // a variable
vars.order.items[0]                  // variables hold any structure
correlationId                        // event correlation ID
message                              // the whole message (payload+attributes)

Inline expressions inside component fields use #[ ... ], e.g. a Logger message of #['Got flight ' ++ attributes.uriParams.ID]. Property placeholders (${http.port}) are resolved at startup and are NOT DataWeave.

Setting event data

ComponentSetsNotes
Transform Messagepayload, any variable, attributesFull DataWeave script per output; the primary transformation tool
Set PayloadpayloadValue field accepts literals or #[expressions]; can set MIME type
Set Variableone variableName + value (literal or expression)
Remove Variable—Deletes a variable from the event

Enrichment with target parameters

Most connector operations expose target and target value parameters. Setting target = flightData (and optionally targetValue = #[payload.data]) stores the operation's result in vars.flightData and leaves the current payload untouched — the Mule 4 replacement for Mule 3's message enricher. Use it whenever you must combine "what I have" with "what another system returns".

Exam tip: after an HTTP Request with target=response, the payload is unchanged and vars.response holds the response body. Without a target, payload = response body and attributes = response metadata. Both facts are heavily tested.

sections.title: Structuring Mule Applications
objectives: Parameterize an application using property placeholders
Define and reuse global configurations in an application
Break an application into multiple flows using flow references, private flows, and subflows
Specify what data is persisted between flows and across a flow reference
Describe the purpose of domain projects
sections.notes:
Project structure

my-app/
├── pom.xml                     <- Maven build (dependencies, plugins)
├── mule-artifact.json          <- app descriptor (minMuleVersion, secureProperties)
└── src/
    ├── main/
    │   ├── mule/               <- Mule configuration XMLs (flows, global elements)
    │   └── resources/          <- properties files, DW modules, examples, log4j2.xml
    └── test/
        ├── munit/              <- MUnit test suites
        └── resources/

Property placeholders

Externalize anything environment-specific (hosts, ports, credentials, paths):

# src/main/resources/config/dev.yaml
db:
  host: "dev-db.acme.com"
  port: "3306"
http:
  listener:
    port: "8081"
  • Register the file with a Configuration properties global element. Parameterize the file name itself to switch environments: config/${env}.yaml, then run with -Denv=dev (in Studio: Run Configuration → VM arguments -Denv=dev -M-Denv=dev pattern for Maven).
  • Reference values as ${db.host} in any configuration field, or p('db.host') inside DataWeave.
  • Precedence: deployment/system properties (-D, Runtime Manager properties) override file values — this is how the same JAR runs in every environment.
  • Secrets should use secure configuration properties (encrypted) — covered in depth in MCD L2, but know they exist and use the secure:: prefix.

Global configurations

Connector configurations (HTTP Listener config, HTTP Request config, Database config…) are global elements: defined once, referenced by any number of operations. Best practice is to keep them in a dedicated config file (e.g. global.xml) so they're easy to find and reuse across the app's XML files — all files are combined at deployment.

Flows, private flows, subflows

FlowPrivate flowSubflow
Event sourceYes (optional but typical)NoNo
Own error handlingYesYesNo — inherits caller's
Invoked bySource or Flow ReferenceFlow ReferenceFlow Reference
Performance——Slightly faster (no extra context)
Callable from DataWeave lookup()YesYesNo

A Flow Reference passes the entire event (payload + attributes + variables) into the referenced flow synchronously. Changes made there (payload, new variables) return to the caller. This is fundamentally different from crossing a transport boundary:

  • App A calls App B over HTTP → only the payload (and headers you set) cross; variables and attributes do NOT.
  • App B receives a brand-new event with its own attributes (from B's HTTP Listener) and empty vars.

Exam tip: "A flow sets var X then calls another flow in the same app via Flow Reference — is X visible?" YES. "…calls another Mule app via HTTP Request — is X visible there?" NO.

Async scope

The Async scope runs its processors in a separate thread and the flow continues immediately — use it for fire-and-forget work (audit logging, notifications) that shouldn't delay the response. The async branch gets a copy of the event; its changes don't come back.

Domain projects

A Mule domain is a special project that holds shared global configurations. Applications deployed to the same customer-hosted runtime can reference one domain and share, e.g., a single HTTP Listener config — meaning several apps share one port. Domains reduce duplicated config and resource usage, but: not supported on CloudHub (each CloudHub app runs isolated on its own worker), all apps must be deployed alongside the domain, and redeploying the domain restarts its apps.

sections.title: Building API Implementation Interfaces
objectives: Manually create a RESTful interface for a Mule application
Generate a REST Connector from a RAML specification
Describe the features and benefits of APIkit
Use APIkit to create implementation flows from a RAML file
Describe how requests are routed through flows generated by APIkit
sections.notes:
Manual RESTful interfaces

You can hand-build an interface: one HTTP Listener per resource/method (paths like /flights/{ID}, listener metadata gives attributes.uriParams), or one listener with wildcard path (/*) plus Choice routers on attributes.requestPath and attributes.method. It works, but you must implement validation, error responses, and routing yourself — and nothing guarantees you stay in sync with the API specification. That is the problem APIkit solves.

APIkit

APIkit is an open-source toolkit that scaffolds a Mule application from an API specification (RAML or OAS; newer versions also support AsyncAPI, GraphQL, OData, SOAP via APIkit for SOAP). Import the spec from Exchange or the filesystem into a Mule project, and scaffolding generates:

  • The main flow: HTTP Listener (default path /api/*) → APIkit Router, plus an error handler with mappings for the standard APIkit errors.
  • One implementation flow per resource/method pair, named by strict convention: get:\flights:american-flights-api-config, get:\flights\(ID):american-flights-api-config, post:\flights:application\json:american-flights-api-config (the media type appears when a request body is declared). The flow name is the routing contract — never rename it.
  • A console flow exposing the interactive API console (default path /console/*).

How the APIkit Router works

  1. Validates each incoming request against the specification: path exists, method allowed, required query params/headers present, body matches the declared type.
  2. Routes valid requests to the implementation flow whose name matches the method + resource (+ media type) + router config name.
  3. Populates attributes.uriParams and attributes.queryParams.
  4. Serializes the flow's output as the HTTP response; sets the status code (200 by default, or whatever the flow sets in attributes / the listener's response builder).

Validation failures raise typed errors mapped by the generated error handler:

APIkit errorHTTP status
APIKIT:BAD_REQUEST400
APIKIT:NOT_FOUND404
APIKIT:METHOD_NOT_ALLOWED405
APIKIT:NOT_ACCEPTABLE406
APIKIT:UNSUPPORTED_MEDIA_TYPE415
APIKIT:NOT_IMPLEMENTED501

Exam tip: a request for a resource that exists in the spec but has no implementation flow yet → the scaffolded flow returns the spec's example (if scaffolded with examples) — while a path that isn't in the spec at all → APIKIT:NOT_FOUND → 404. A missing required query param → APIKIT:BAD_REQUEST → 400.

Keeping spec and implementation in sync

  • Update the RAML → right-click the API in Studio → Scaffold flows from these API specifications: new resource/method pairs get new flows; existing implementation flows are preserved.
  • The spec can be kept as a design library dependency from Exchange, so implementation always tracks the published contract version.

REST Connect connectors

When an API specification is published to Exchange, the platform auto-generates a REST Connector (REST Connect). Consumers add it from Exchange to their Mule project and get one typed operation per method — with fields for parameters and metadata for DataSense — instead of hand-configuring HTTP Request URLs. If the connector can't be generated, consumers can still download the spec or call the API with plain HTTP Request.

sections.title: Routing Events
objectives: Use the Choice router to route events based on conditional logic
Use the Scatter-Gather router to multicast events
Validate data using the Validation module
sections.notes:
Choice router — conditional, exactly one route

<choice>
  <when expression="#[payload.destination == 'SFO']">
    ... route 1 ...
  </when>
  <when expression="#[payload.destination == 'LAX']">
    ... route 2 ...
  </when>
  <otherwise>
    ... default route ...
  </otherwise>
</choice>
  • Routes are evaluated top to bottom; the first expression returning true wins — order your conditions from most to least specific.
  • If nothing matches and there is no otherwise, the event continues unchanged past the router.
  • Equivalent to if / else-if / else. For value-based transformation, a DataWeave if/else or match inside a Transform is often cleaner than a Choice.

Scatter-Gather — concurrent multicast

  • Sends a copy of the event to every route concurrently (minimum two routes) and waits for all to finish (configurable timeout).
  • The result is a new payload: an object keyed by route index, each entry holding that route's full Mule message:
{
  "0": { "attributes": {...}, "payload": [ ...route 0 result... ] },
  "1": { "attributes": {...}, "payload": [ ...route 1 result... ] }
}

Typical aggregation after the router:

%dw 2.0
output application/json
---
flatten(payload pluck $.payload)   // merge all route payloads into one array
  • Error behavior: all routes still run; if any fail, the router raises MULE:COMPOSITE_ROUTING aggregating each route's error. To tolerate partial failure, put a Try scope (with On Error Continue) inside the failing-prone routes.
  • Use when independent calls can run in parallel (e.g. query three airline systems and combine results) — total time ≈ slowest route, not the sum.

Other routers (know they exist)

RouterBehavior
First SuccessfulTries routes in order until one completes without error (failover pattern)
Round RobinOne route per event, rotating through routes (simple load distribution)

Validation module

Validators check a condition and, on failure, throw a Mule error in the VALIDATION namespace (they don't return booleans). This "fail fast with a typed error" style pairs naturally with error handlers and APIkit (map to 400 responses).

  • Common validators: Is true / Is false, Is not blank string / Is blank string, Is not null / Is null, Is number, Is email, Is IP, Matches regex, Is elapsed, Is not elapsed.
  • Each validator lets you customize the error message; the raised type is specific, e.g. VALIDATION:INVALID_BOOLEAN, VALIDATION:BLANK_STRING, VALIDATION:INVALID_EMAIL.
  • All / Any validators combine several validations into one result.

Exam tip: "Route a copy of the message to ALL of these systems" → Scatter-Gather. "Route to ONE system depending on a field" → Choice. "Reject requests with an invalid email, returning a clear error" → Validation + error handler. Also remember Scatter-Gather's output shape — questions love asking what the payload looks like afterwards.

sections.title: Handling Errors
objectives: Describe the default error handling in a Mule application
Define a custom global default error handler and identify when it is used
Compare and contrast On Error Continue and On Error Propagate scopes
Handle errors at the application, flow, and processor level
Describe the data structure of the Mule Error object
Map errors to custom application errors
sections.notes:
Two kinds of errors

  • System errors — occur outside event processing (app startup failure, a connection lost while idle). No Mule event is involved, so no error handler can catch them; Mule logs them and applies reconnection strategies.
  • Messaging errors — thrown while an event is processed in a flow. These are what you handle with error handlers.

The Mule Error object

When a processor fails, execution jumps to error handling and the event gains an error object:

FieldContents
error.errorTypeNamespace + identifier, e.g. HTTP:NOT_FOUND, VALIDATION:INVALID_EMAIL
error.descriptionHuman-readable description of what failed
error.causeThe underlying Java exception
error.errorMessageFor some connectors, a Mule message describing the failing response (e.g. the HTTP error body)
error.childErrorsAggregated errors (e.g. from Scatter-Gather composite routing)

Error types form a hierarchy. Every error extends MULE:ANY. Examples: HTTP:NOT_FOUND and HTTP:CONNECTIVITY are HTTP-namespace types; HTTP:CONNECTIVITY also extends MULE:CONNECTIVITY. Handling a parent type catches all of its children. Special types: MULE:UNKNOWN (unmatchable except by ANY), MULE:CRITICAL (not handleable at all).

Default behavior (no error handler anywhere)

Mule applies the built-in default error handler, which propagates the error: it is logged, the flow stops, and the source gets the error response — an HTTP Listener returns 500 with the error description.

On Error Propagate vs On Error Continue

On Error PropagateOn Error Continue
After handler runsRe-throws the error upContinues as if the flow succeeded
Rest of the flowSkippedSkipped (handler output becomes the flow result)
HTTP Listener returnsError response (500 by default) — body can be customized in the handlerSuccess response (200) with the handler's payload
When called via Flow ReferenceError surfaces in the calling flow at the Flow ReferenceCaller continues normally after the Flow Reference

Each On Error scope can match on: type (one or more error types, comma-separated) and/or when (a DataWeave boolean, e.g. #[error.errorType.identifier == 'NOT_FOUND']). Within an error handler, scopes are checked in order; first match wins — put specific types before general ones. An unmatched error behaves as if the handler didn't exist (bubbles to the next level).

Three levels of handling

  1. Processor level — Try scope. Wrap one or more processors; attach its own error handler. Classic use: let one optional call fail (On Error Continue in the Try) while the rest of the flow proceeds.
  2. Flow level. Each flow (and private flow) has an error handling section with its own On Error scopes.
  3. Application level — global default error handler. Define a global error-handler element and set it as the app's default in the configuration (Global Elements → Configuration → Default Error Handler). It applies only to flows that define no error handling of their own — it's a fallback, never an addition. Subflows never have their own handling; they always use the caller's context.

Raising and mapping errors

  • Raise Error component throws a custom error explicitly: type like APP:ORDER_INVALID plus a description — useful after a business rule check.
  • Error mapping (on any connector operation, "Error Mapping" tab) converts a source type into a custom one: e.g. map HTTP:NOT_FOUND from a customer-service call to APP:CUSTOMER_NOT_FOUND. Handlers can then react to business-meaningful types, and two different calls that both might 404 become distinguishable.

Exam tip: the most-tested scenarios: (1) which HTTP status the client sees for Continue vs Propagate; (2) what happens in the CALLING flow when a referenced flow's handler is Continue vs Propagate; (3) first-match-wins ordering with MULE:ANY placed first (it swallows everything). Trace them slowly — they're free points once you know the rules.

sections.title: Transforming Data with DataWeave
objectives: Write DataWeave scripts to convert JSON, XML, and Java data structures
Use DataWeave functions (including map, filter, orderBy, groupBy)
Define and use DataWeave variables, functions, and custom data types
Use DataWeave modules and coerce/format strings, numbers, and dates
Call Mule flows from DataWeave scripts (lookup)
sections.notes:
Script anatomy

%dw 2.0                          // header: version
output application/json          // header: output format
import * from dw::core::Strings // header: imports
var taxRate = 1.23               // header: variables
fun withTax(n: Number) = n * taxRate   // header: functions
type Money = Number { format: "#,##0.00" } // header: custom types
---
payload map (item, index) -> {   // body: ONE expression
  id: index,
  total: withTax(item.price) as Money
}

Output formats: application/json, application/xml, application/java, application/csv, text/plain… The body is a single expression; everything reusable goes in the header.

Selectors

SelectorExampleReturns
Single valuepayload.flight.priceValue of the key
Index / rangepayload[0], payload[1 to 3], payload[-1]Element(s); -1 is the last
Multi-valuepayload.*flightArray of ALL matching keys (essential for repeated XML elements)
Descendantspayload..priceAll 'price' values at any depth
XML attributepayload.flight.@codeAttribute value
Key existencepayload.customer?true/false

Core functions (dw::Core is auto-imported)

payload map (f) -> { code: f.code }        // transform each array element ($, $$ = value, index)
payload filter ($.price < 500)             // keep matching elements
payload orderBy $.price                    // ascending sort
(payload orderBy $.price)[-1 to 0]         // descending (reverse)
payload groupBy $.destination              // object of arrays keyed by criteria
payload distinctBy $.code                  // remove duplicates
payload reduce ((item, acc = 0) -> acc + item.price)   // fold to a single value
flatten([[1,2],[3]])                       // [1,2,3]
payload.book mapObject (v, k) -> { (upper(k)): v }     // transform object entries
payload pluck (v, k) -> { key: k, value: v }           // object -> array
"a,b,c" splitBy ","    /  ["a","b"] joinBy "-"
sizeOf(payload)  /  isEmpty(payload)  /  payload contains "SFO"
if (payload.price > 100) "expensive" else "cheap"
payload.customer.name default "Anonymous"  // fallback for null

Concatenate with ++ (strings, arrays, objects). String helpers live in dw::core::Strings (capitalize, camelize…), array helpers in dw::core::Arrays.

Working with XML

  • XML output requires a single root element; multiple top-level keys fail.
  • Repeated elements: reading — use * (payload.flights.*flight); writing from an array inside an object, wrap with {( ... )}:
%dw 2.0
output application/xml
---
flights: {
  (payload map (f) -> flight @(code: f.code): { price: f.price })
}
  • @(name: value) writes XML attributes; payload.el.@name reads them. Namespaces are declared in the header with ns.
  • CSV: reader/writer properties like header=false, separator='|' are set on the format line: output application/csv separator="|".

Type coercion and formatting

"123" as Number                          // 123
price as String {format: "###.00"}       // "1250.00"
"2024-03-15" as Date                     // Date object
now() as String {format: "yyyy-MM-dd"}   // formatted timestamp
"15/03/2024" as Date {format: "dd/MM/yyyy"}  // parse a non-ISO date
now() + |P1D|                            // date arithmetic with periods
|2024-03-15| as String {format: "MMM d"} // date literal

Common coercion chain for reformatting a date string: parse with one format (as Date {format: ...}) then output with another (as String {format: ...}).

Variables, functions, modules, lookup

  • var/fun in the header; do { ... } creates local scopes inside the body.
  • Reusable libraries: a .dwl file in src/main/resources (e.g. modules/Utils.dwl) → import modules::Utils then Utils::myFun(x), or import * from modules::Utils for unqualified use.
  • lookup("flowName", payload) invokes a flow (not a subflow) and returns its payload — handy but use sparingly (harder error handling, hidden coupling).

Exam tip: practice reading scripts and predicting output shape: map → array, groupBy → object of arrays, pluck → array, mapObject → object, reduce → single value. And remember $=value, $$=key/index.

sections.title: Using Connectors
objectives: Retrieve data from a database with the Database connector, including parameterized queries
Retrieve data from a REST service using HTTP Request or a REST Connector
Use a Web Service Consumer to consume SOAP web services
Use the Transform Message component to pass parameters to a SOAP web service
List, read, and write files with the File and FTP connectors
Publish and consume JMS messages
sections.notes:
Database connector

  • Operations: Select, Insert, Update, Delete, Bulk insert/update/delete, Stored procedure, Execute DDL; sources: On Table Row (polling with watermark).
  • The JDBC driver is a Maven dependency (Studio adds it via the config's "Configure" button). Connection can be generic (URL + driver class) or vendor-specific; supports connection pooling.
  • Always parameterize queries — input parameters prevent SQL injection and handle escaping/typing:
SELECT * FROM american
WHERE toAirport = :destination AND price < :maxPrice
-- Input parameters:
#[{ destination: attributes.queryParams.destination,
    maxPrice: 500 }]
  • Select returns an array of objects (one per row) — typically followed by a Transform. Insert/Update return affected-row counts and generated keys in the payload/attributes.

HTTP Request & REST connectors

  • Config holds base host/port/protocol (parameterize with properties); the operation sets method, path, headers, query params (fields or a DataWeave map), and body (current payload by default).
  • Response validation: only 2xx (and 3xx per config) count as success; a 404 raises HTTP:NOT_FOUND, a 500 raises HTTP:INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR, connection failures raise HTTP:CONNECTIVITY. The "success status codes" setting can widen what's accepted (e.g. 200..404 to handle 404 without errors).
  • After the call: payload = response body, attributes = status code + reason + headers (the previous attributes are gone!).
  • A REST Connector from Exchange (REST Connect) is the typed alternative: one operation per method with DataSense metadata. Authentication schemes (basic, OAuth, client id enforcement headers) configured on the connector config.

Web Service Consumer (SOAP)

  • Configure with the WSDL (location, service, port, address) — the connector exposes each SOAP operation.
  • The payload sent must be the SOAP body XML for the operation. Because the connector publishes metadata from the WSDL, a Transform Message placed before the Consume operation shows the expected XML structure as output metadata — map your data to it (namespaces included).
  • SOAP faults raise errors (WSC:SOAP_FAULT); headers can be set via the operation's "headers" DataWeave.

File / FTP / SFTP connectors

  • Operations: List, Read, Write, Copy, Move, Delete, Create directory; source: On New or Updated File (polls a directory).
  • Listener best practices: set post-processing — Move to another directory and/or rename, or auto-delete — so the same file isn't reprocessed; use matching rules (filename pattern) to select files. Watermark mode (file timestamps) can restrict to new/updated files.
  • Write operation: path + content (defaults to payload); modes: OVERWRITE, APPEND, CREATE_NEW. List returns an array of file messages; each message's attributes carry file name/size/timestamps.
  • FTP/SFTP mirror the File operations over remote protocols (SFTP = SSH; FTPS = TLS).

JMS connector

  • Messaging styles: queues = point-to-point (one consumer receives each message), topics = publish/subscribe (every subscriber gets a copy).
  • Operations: Publish, Consume (one message, on demand), Publish-Consume (request-reply over queues); source: On New Message (listener).
  • Why messaging: decouples producer from consumer in time and availability, buffers load spikes, enables async processing and reliable delivery (acknowledgements, persistent messages, transactions).
  • Message = body + properties (custom headers) + JMS headers (correlation id, reply-to). Attributes after Consume/On New Message expose these.

Exam tip: match the connector to the scenario: database table sync → Database + On Table Row; existing SOAP service → Web Service Consumer (never HTTP Request); nightly CSV drop in a folder → File listener with move-after-processing; decouple two flows/apps with buffering → JMS (or VM within one app).

sections.title: Processing Records
objectives: List and compare the methods for processing individual records in a collection
Use the For Each scope to process records
Use the Batch Job scope (with Batch Steps and Batch Aggregator) to process records
Use the Scheduler component and connector listeners to trigger flows
Use watermarking (automatic and manual) to synchronize data
Persist and share data using the Object Store
sections.notes:
Choosing a record-processing tool

For EachParallel For EachBatch Job
ExecutionSequential, one at a timeConcurrentParallel, queued, async
OutputOriginal payload (changes inside discarded)Array of each iteration's resultBatchJobResult (in On Complete)
On record errorStops processing (unless handled)Collects failures; composite errorConfigurable (maxFailedRecords); record-level tolerance
Persistence / restartNoNoYes — records go to persistent queues; job resumes after crash
Best forSmall collections, side effects in orderIndependent medium workloadsVery large datasets, near-real-time sync, reliability (EE only)

For Each notes: splits on #[payload] by default (configurable collection expression); batch size option groups elements; variables set inside persist after the scope, payload changes do not.

Batch Job — three phases

  1. Load and Dispatch (implicit): splits the input (Java Iterables/Iterators/Arrays, JSON or XML arrays) into records and queues them into a persistent batch queue, creating the batch job instance. The flow that triggered the job continues immediately (the original payload moves on; the job runs asynchronously).
  2. Process: each record moves through the Batch Steps in parallel (block size defaults to 100 records per thread batch). Per step:
    • Accept Expression — DataWeave condition a record must satisfy to enter the step.
    • Accept Policy — ALL, NO_FAILURES (default), ONLY_FAILURES — a final step with ONLY_FAILURES implements per-record error reporting.
    • Batch Aggregator (optional, inside a step) — accumulates records into arrays of fixed size (e.g. bulk upsert 100 at a time) or streaming (all records, one pass).
    • Record-level variables set in one step are visible for the same record in later steps; variables from the Process phase do not reach On Complete.
  3. On Complete: runs once; payload is a BatchJobResult — totalRecords, processedRecords, successfulRecords, failedRecords, loadedRecords… Use it to log/report. The original payload is NOT available here.

Error tolerance: maxFailedRecords = 0 (default) stops the job at the first failed record; -1 tolerates unlimited failures; N stops after N. Failed records skip remaining steps unless a step's accept policy includes failures.

Triggering flows

  • Scheduler: Fixed frequency (every N ms/sec/min, with start delay) or Cron expression (e.g. 0 0 2 * * ? = 02:00 daily). If a run is still executing when the next tick fires, the new one is skipped for that flow instance.
  • Connector listeners: On New or Updated File, On New Message (JMS/VM/Anypoint MQ), On Table Row (Database) — event-driven alternatives to polling with a Scheduler.

Watermarking — sync only what's new

  • Automatic: built into some sources. On Table Row: choose a watermark column (incrementing id or timestamp) — the connector persists the max seen value and adds it to the query filter each poll. On New or Updated File: timestamp-based watermark option.
  • Manual: when logic is custom (e.g. Scheduler + HTTP "get changes since X"): read the stored watermark from an Object Store, query with it, then store the new max. Components: os:retrieve (with defaultValue), os:store.

Object Store

  • Key/value persistence per app: operations Store, Retrieve (with default), Contains, Remove, Clear; entries can have TTL. Backing: in-memory or persistent; on CloudHub, Object Store v2.
  • Used by: manual watermarks, Cache scope, Idempotent Message Validator, custom state (counters, tokens).

Exam tip: classic questions — "what is the payload after For Each?" (the original), "where do you see failed record counts?" (On Complete's BatchJobResult), "how do you process only failed records in the last step?" (accept policy ONLY_FAILURES), and "sync new records hourly without duplicates" (watermarking).

sections.title: Debugging and Troubleshooting Mule Applications
objectives: Use breakpoints to inspect a Mule event at runtime
Install missing Maven dependencies
Read and decipher Mule log error messages
sections.notes:
The Mule debugger

  • Right-click a processor → Add breakpoint, then Debug As → Mule Application. The debugger connects on port 6666 by default (changeable in the debug configuration).
  • When paused, the Mule Debugger view shows the event as it arrives at the breakpointed processor: payload, attributes, variables, error (if any).
  • The expression evaluator runs arbitrary DataWeave against the paused event (e.g. test payload.items filter $.qty > 0 before writing it into a transform).
  • Step controls: step over / step into (e.g. into a referenced flow) / resume. Careful with streams: viewing a streamed payload in the debugger can consume it.
  • Breakpoints can be conditional (a DataWeave expression) and can be set on exception (break when an error is raised).

Maven dependency problems

  • Symptoms: build fails with Could not resolve dependencies / ClassNotFoundException at runtime / Studio error markers on connector operations.
  • Fixes: declare the missing artifact in pom.xml (for JDBC drivers, Studio's Database config UI offers to add it); check the version exists; verify the repository is reachable and credentials in settings.xml are valid (needed for Exchange-hosted assets); force a refresh with mvn clean install -U or Studio → right-click project → Mule → Update project dependencies.
  • Connector dependencies from Exchange are added automatically when dragged from the Mule Palette ("Search in Exchange").

Reading Mule logs

Logging is Log4j 2 (src/main/resources/log4j2.xml); Studio console shows the same output as logs/mule_ee.log style files. A log line contains: timestamp, level (ERROR/WARN/INFO/DEBUG/TRACE), thread, logger category, event correlation id, and message. A typical error block:

ERROR 2026-05-01 10:15:32,481 [[MuleRuntime].uber.04: [training-app].get:\flights:api-config...]
********************************************************************
Message               : HTTP GET on resource 'http://acme:8081/api/flights' failed: not found (404)
Error type            : HTTP:NOT_FOUND
Element               : get:\flights:api-config/processors/1 @ training-app
...(stack trace)...
  • Message + Error type tell you what; Element tells you which processor in which flow. Read these before the stack trace.
  • The thread name reveals the flow; the correlation id groups all lines of one event's journey.
  • Logger component: set message (#[...] expressions), level, and category (e.g. com.acme.flights) — categories are filtered/leveled in log4j2.xml. Logging a streamed payload prints a cursor object (e.g. ManagedCursorStreamProvider) unless converted: #[payload as String] or transform first — and beware that doing so consumes/loads the stream.
  • Common startup errors: port already in use (another app on 8081), invalid listener path, unresolved property placeholder (Couldn't find configuration property value for key).

Exam tip: given a log excerpt, identify the failing component and error type. Remember: the debugger shows the event BEFORE the selected processor runs, and the default debugger port is 6666.

sections.title: Deploying and Managing APIs
objectives: Package Mule applications for deployment
Deploy applications to CloudHub
Use API Manager to create and deploy API proxies
Connect an API implementation to API Manager using autodiscovery
Restrict access with policies, client ID enforcement, and SLA tiers
sections.notes:
Packaging

  • A Mule 4 app packages as a deployable JAR: mvn clean package (or Studio → Export → Anypoint Studio Project to Mule Deployable Archive). It bundles the app XMLs, resources, and dependencies; mule-artifact.json declares minMuleVersion and secure properties.
  • The same JAR deploys to any target: CloudHub, Runtime Fabric, or customer-hosted runtimes (hybrid). Configuration differences come from properties, not rebuilds.

CloudHub deployment

  • Deploy from Studio (Anypoint Platform → Deploy), Runtime Manager (upload JAR), Anypoint CLI, or Maven. Choose: region, runtime version, worker size (0.1 / 0.2 / 1 / 2 / 4 … vCores) and number of workers.
  • Vertical scaling = bigger workers; horizontal scaling = more workers with automatic load balancing across them. Multiple workers also give high availability.
  • App URL: <appname>.<region>.cloudhub.io; app names are unique per region. Set/override properties in Runtime Manager (Settings → Properties) — values there beat the packaged files; mark sensitive ones as hidden.
  • Runtime Manager also provides: logs, restart/stop, static IPs, schedules management (manage Scheduler-triggered flows), and alerts (app down, deployment failed…).

Managing APIs — API Manager

API Manager governs API instances (an API spec version deployed to an environment). Two ways to bring an implementation under management:

API proxyAutodiscovery (basic endpoint)
What runsA separate proxy app generated and deployed by API Manager in front of the implementationThe implementation itself registers with API Manager
Where policies executeIn the proxyInside the implementation app
Code changesNoneAdd an API Autodiscovery global element
Use whenImplementation can't be modified / non-Mule backendMule implementation you control (avoids extra hop)

Autodiscovery requirements: the app declares <api-gateway:autodiscovery apiId="${api.id}" flowRef="main-flow"/> where the apiId matches the API instance in API Manager for THAT environment, and the runtime has Anypoint Platform client credentials (client id/secret of the environment, set as properties anypoint.platform.client_id/client_secret — automatic on CloudHub deployments from the same org). Status turns green ("Active") in API Manager when paired; policies are then pulled and enforced by the app.

Policies

PolicyPurpose
Rate limiting / Rate limiting SLA-basedCap requests per window (globally, or per client tier)
Spike controlSmooth bursts (queue/delay instead of hard reject)
Client ID enforcementRequire client_id/client_secret from registered consumers
OAuth 2.0 / JWT validation / Basic authAuthentication and authorization
IP allowlist / blocklistNetwork-level restriction
CORS, Header injection/removalCross-origin support, header manipulation
  • Policies are applied per API instance in API Manager — no redeployment of the app is needed; gateways poll and apply them. Custom policies can be published to Exchange.
  • Resource-level policies can target specific methods/paths instead of the whole API.

Client ID enforcement, contracts and SLA tiers

  1. Define SLA tiers on the API instance (e.g. Gold: 1000 req/min auto-approved; Silver: 100 req/min manual approval).
  2. A consumer finds the API in Exchange and requests access with (or creating) a client application, choosing a tier → this creates a contract once approved, and the client app gets client_id + client_secret.
  3. With client ID enforcement (or SLA-based rate limiting) applied, requests must carry the credentials (headers or query params per policy config, e.g. client_id/client_secret headers); the gateway identifies the consumer, checks the contract, and applies the tier's limits. Violations → 401 (invalid credentials) or 429 (over the limit).

Exam tip: know the request-access flow order (Exchange → client app → tier → contract → credentials) and that SLA-based rate limiting REQUIRES a client-identifying policy. Also: policies apply per environment-specific API instance, and changing policies never requires redeploying the Mule app.

cards.front: What are the three layers of API-led connectivity, top to bottom?
cards.back: Experience APIs (channel-specific) → Process APIs (orchestration, business logic) → System APIs (unlock systems of record).

cards.front: C4E vs traditional CoE — key difference?
cards.back: A C4E ENABLES other teams (publishes reusable assets, best practices, measures consumption). A CoE centralizes delivery and becomes a bottleneck.

cards.front: Which HTTP methods are idempotent?
cards.back: GET, PUT, DELETE (also HEAD/OPTIONS). POST is NOT idempotent.

cards.front: HTTP 401 vs 403?
cards.back: 401 Unauthorized = not authenticated (bad/missing credentials). 403 Forbidden = authenticated but not allowed.

cards.front: Where do consumers discover APIs and request access? Where are policies applied?
cards.back: Discover/request access: Anypoint Exchange. Apply policies: API Manager.

cards.front: In RAML, when do you use a URI parameter vs a query parameter?
cards.back: URI parameter {id} identifies a SPECIFIC resource (/flights/{id}). Query parameter filters/sorts/paginates a collection (?destination=SFO).

cards.front: RAML: trait vs resourceType?
cards.back: Trait = reusable METHOD-level pattern, applied with 'is:'. ResourceType = reusable RESOURCE-level pattern, applied with 'type:'.

cards.front: What does the mocking service do?
cards.back: Serves responses straight from the RAML/OAS examples so consumers can try the API before any implementation exists.

cards.front: What are the three parts of a Mule event?
cards.back: Mule message (payload + attributes) and variables. The event is immutable — each change creates a new instance.

cards.front: What happens to attributes after ANY connector operation (e.g., HTTP Request)?
cards.back: The whole message is replaced: payload = operation result AND attributes = metadata of that result. Original attributes are gone — save them to a var first.

cards.front: What does setting 'target' on a connector operation do?
cards.back: Stores the operation result in vars.<target> and leaves the current payload UNCHANGED (message enrichment).

cards.front: DataWeave: how do you read query param 'code' and URI param 'id'?
cards.back: attributes.queryParams.code and attributes.uriParams.id

cards.front: Property precedence: packaged file vs deployment/system property?
cards.back: Deployment and system properties (-D, Runtime Manager) OVERRIDE packaged property files — that's how one JAR serves all environments.

cards.front: Flow vs private flow vs subflow — error handling and sources?
cards.back: Flow: source + own error handling. Private flow: no source, own error handling. Subflow: no source, NO error handling (inherits caller's), slightly faster.

cards.front: Do variables cross a Flow Reference? An HTTP call to another app?
cards.back: Flow Reference: YES (whole event travels, changes return). HTTP to another app: NO — only the payload/headers cross the transport boundary.

cards.front: Where are Mule domains supported and what do they share?
cards.back: Customer-hosted runtimes only (NOT CloudHub). They share global configs — e.g., one HTTP listener/port across apps on the same runtime.

cards.front: APIkit flow naming convention for GET /flights with config 'api-config'?
cards.back: get:\flights:api-config — the name IS the routing contract; renaming breaks routing (→ 501 NOT_IMPLEMENTED).

cards.front: APIKIT error → HTTP status: BAD_REQUEST, NOT_FOUND, METHOD_NOT_ALLOWED, UNSUPPORTED_MEDIA_TYPE?
cards.back: 400, 404, 405, 415 (also NOT_ACCEPTABLE→406, NOT_IMPLEMENTED→501).

cards.front: What is REST Connect?
cards.back: When a REST spec is published to Exchange, a connector is auto-generated (one operation per method) that can be used in Mule apps instead of raw HTTP Request.

cards.front: Choice router: which route runs if several conditions are true?
cards.back: The FIRST matching route (top to bottom) — order conditions from most to least specific. No match + no otherwise = event continues unchanged.

cards.front: Scatter-Gather: execution model and resulting payload?
cards.back: All routes run CONCURRENTLY on a copy of the event; waits for all. Result = object keyed by route index ('0','1',...), each holding that route's full message.

cards.front: What do Validation module components do on failure?
cards.back: They THROW a typed error in the VALIDATION namespace (e.g., VALIDATION:INVALID_EMAIL). They don't return booleans.

cards.front: On Error Continue vs On Error Propagate — HTTP response to the client?
cards.back: Continue: flow 'succeeds' → 200 with the handler's payload. Propagate: error re-thrown → error response (500 by default).

cards.front: Which error fields exist on the Mule error object?
cards.back: error.errorType (NAMESPACE:IDENTIFIER), error.description, error.cause, error.errorMessage (e.g., HTTP error body), error.childErrors.

cards.front: When does the app-level default error handler apply?
cards.back: Only for flows that define NO error handling of their own. It's a fallback, never an addition.

cards.front: System error vs messaging error?
cards.back: System: outside event processing (startup, idle connection lost) — NOT catchable by handlers. Messaging: during flow processing — handled by error handlers.

cards.front: DataWeave: output type of map, groupBy, pluck, mapObject, reduce?
cards.back: map → Array; groupBy → Object of arrays; pluck → Array; mapObject → Object; reduce → single value.

cards.front: DataWeave lambda shorthand: what are $ and $$ for arrays and for objects?
cards.back: Arrays: $ = value, $$ = index. Objects (mapObject/pluck): $ = value, $$ = key.

cards.front: Reformat '25/12/2025' to '2025-12-25' in DataWeave?
cards.back: ('25/12/2025' as Date {format: 'dd/MM/yyyy'}) as String {format: 'yyyy-MM-dd'} — parse with input format, output with target format.

cards.front: Can lookup() call a subflow?
cards.back: No — lookup('flowName', payload) invokes FLOWS only (and returns the payload). Subflows are not callable from DataWeave.

cards.front: Why parameterize DB queries with :params?
cards.back: Prevents SQL injection and handles escaping/typing — input parameters separate SQL from data.

cards.front: JMS queue vs topic?
cards.back: Queue = point-to-point: each message to exactly ONE consumer. Topic = pub-sub: every subscriber gets a COPY.

cards.front: What is the payload AFTER a For Each? And what survives from inside it?
cards.back: The ORIGINAL payload (changes inside are discarded); variables set inside DO persist.

cards.front: Batch job: what payload does On Complete receive, and what is maxFailedRecords' default?
cards.back: A BatchJobResult (totalRecords, successfulRecords, failedRecords...) — NOT the original payload. maxFailedRecords defaults to 0 (stop on first failure); -1 = tolerate all.

cards.front: Batch step accept policies?
cards.back: ALL, NO_FAILURES (default), ONLY_FAILURES — a final step with ONLY_FAILURES handles failed records.

cards.front: Default Mule debugger port? What does a breakpoint show?
cards.back: Port 6666. The event AS IT ARRIVES at the selected processor (before it executes).

cards.front: API proxy vs autodiscovery (basic endpoint)?
cards.back: Proxy: separate app in front, policies run there — for backends you can't modify. Autodiscovery: the Mule implementation registers itself, policies run inside it — no extra hop.

cards.front: Client sends invalid client_id vs exceeds its SLA tier — response codes?
cards.back: Invalid credentials → 401. Over the tier limit → 429 Too Many Requests.
